author: Alkarim Billawala / alkarim.billawala.ca
weeks: Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man is referred after his dentist notes jaw widening (prognathism). His shoe size and ring size have increased, and he has new T2DM and carpal tunnel syndrome. What is the best initial screening test?
questions.explain: Acromegaly screening is IGF-1 (a stable integrated marker of GH action). Random GH is too pulsatile. Confirmation is failure of GH to suppress after an oral glucose load; MRI follows biochemical confirmation.

questions.stem: A 26-year-old woman has 4 months of amenorrhea and new galactorrhea. She recently started metoclopramide for nausea. β-hCG negative, TSH 1.5, prolactin 190 µg/L. What is the most appropriate next step?
questions.explain: Metoclopramide is a dopamine antagonist that removes tonic lactotroph inhibition, raising prolactin (often up to ~200 µg/L). With normal TSH and a clear drug cause, stop the offending drug and recheck before imaging or a dopamine agonist.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old has an incidental 3 cm pituitary macroadenoma, no galactorrhea, and a prolactin of only 150 µg/L. The clinician suspects the true prolactin is much higher. Which phenomenon explains the falsely low reading?
options: Hook effect (assay saturation by very high prolactin)
questions.explain: The hook effect: extremely high prolactin overwhelms the assay antibodies and is reported falsely low. (The stalk effect instead explains a mild rise <200 from a non-functioning mass compressing the stalk and blocking dopamine.)

questions.stem: A 45-year-old reports walking into door frames. Confrontation fields show bitemporal hemianopsia; the swinging-flashlight test makes both pupils dilate when light moves to the left eye. What is being compressed?
questions.explain: Bitemporal hemianopsia + a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) localizes to the optic chiasm/nerve (CN II), classically compressed by a pituitary adenoma extending out of the sella.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old, 8 weeks pregnant, has palpitations, heat intolerance, weight loss, a diffuse non-tender goiter, suppressed TSH, high fT4, and positive TRAb. Which treatment and which severe side effect to warn about?
questions.explain: First-trimester Graves' is treated with PTU (methimazole is teratogenic early). Warn about agranulocytosis — fever or sore throat means stop the drug and get an urgent CBC. RAI is contraindicated in pregnancy.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man has weight loss, palpitations, new atrial fibrillation, a palpable right thyroid nodule, suppressed TSH, and high fT4. What is the best next test, and what result avoids an FNA?
questions.explain: With suppressed TSH, get a RAIU scan. A focal hot nodule (toxic adenoma) explains the thyrotoxicosis and is almost never malignant, so FNA is unnecessary. Cold nodules are the ones needing FNA.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman has 2 weeks of palpitations and a tender thyroid after a viral illness. TSH suppressed, fT4 high, and RAIU is LOW. What is the best management?
questions.explain: Low uptake = subacute thyroiditis (pre-formed hormone leak), not new synthesis — thionamides don't help. Manage symptomatically with NSAIDs and a beta-blocker; it is self-limited (may pass through a hypothyroid phase).

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man with stable angina is found to have TSH 14, fT4 low. How should levothyroxine be started?
options: Start low (25–50 mcg) and titrate slowly
questions.explain: In the elderly or those with cardiac disease, start low (25–50 mcg) and go slow — a sudden rise in metabolic rate can precipitate angina or arrhythmia. Recheck TSH in 6 weeks.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with Hashimoto's, started on levothyroxine 2 weeks ago, presents with profound fatigue, hypotension, nausea, and hyperpigmented palmar creases and buccal mucosa. What happened and why the pigmentation?
options: Addisonian crisis unmasked by levothyroxine; high ACTH/MSH from POMC
questions.explain: Starting levothyroxine raised metabolic/cortisol demand and unmasked primary adrenal insufficiency → Addisonian crisis. Hyperpigmentation is from high ACTH, which shares the POMC precursor with MSH and stimulates melanocortin receptors.

questions.stem: A 24-year-old man with suspected primary adrenal insufficiency is hypotensive. You plan an ACTH stimulation test but must treat now. Which IV glucocorticoid stabilizes him without interfering with the cortisol assay?
questions.explain: Dexamethasone does not cross-react with the cortisol assay, so it stabilizes the patient while the ACTH-stimulation test is completed. (Hydrocortisone is standard once testing is done or not planned.)

questions.stem: A 42-year-old woman has central weight gain, purple striae, and proximal weakness; she denies steroids. 24-h urine free cortisol is high and 1-mg dexamethasone fails to suppress. ACTH is 0.5 pg/mL (low). Where is the lesion?
questions.explain: Confirmed endogenous Cushing's with a low ACTH = ACTH-independent → autonomous cortisol from an adrenal adenoma/carcinoma suppressing pituitary ACTH. High/normal ACTH would point to pituitary or ectopic sources.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old woman has refractory hypertension (180/110) and K 2.4 mmol/L, with low renin and high aldosterone. Which medication class directly blocks the epithelial sodium channel for this condition?
questions.explain: Primary aldosteronism (Conn's): aldosterone antagonists (spironolactone/eplerenone) or an ENaC blocker — amiloride — are the medical options. Amiloride directly blocks the collecting-duct sodium channel, sparing potassium.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old has episodic headaches, palpitations, and sweating with paroxysmal hypertension; CT shows a 2.9 cm hypervascular adrenal mass (40 HU) and urinary metanephrines are high. What pre-op pharmacologic sequence is required?
questions.explain: Pheochromocytoma needs alpha-blockade first (doxazosin/phenoxybenzamine), then beta-blockade. Giving a beta-blocker first leaves unopposed alpha vasoconstriction → hypertensive crisis. Skin findings (café-au-lait, neurofibromas, mucosal neuromas) hint at NF1/MEN2B.

questions.stem: A 31-year-old woman with oligomenorrhea and infertility has prolactin 95 µg/L and TSH 16.0 (negative pregnancy test, no meds). What links the two, and what is the best initial therapy?
options: Primary hypothyroidism → high TRH stimulates prolactin; levothyroxine
questions.explain: Primary hypothyroidism raises TRH, which directly stimulates lactotrophs → mild hyperprolactinemia. Treat the hypothyroidism with levothyroxine; prolactin normalizes — no dopamine agonist needed.

questions.stem: Which electrolyte pattern is most characteristic of primary adrenal insufficiency?
questions.explain: Aldosterone deficiency causes sodium loss and potassium retention → hyponatremia + hyperkalemia (with hypotension and hypoglycemia). This pattern in a hyperpigmented, hypotensive patient points to Addison's.

questions.stem: A thyrotoxic patient develops fever, agitation, and high-output heart failure after surgery. Which treatment ordering is correct for thyroid storm?
options: Beta-blocker + PTU, then iodine, plus glucocorticoids
questions.explain: Thyroid storm: beta-blocker + thionamide (PTU) first, then iodine (given after the thionamide so it doesn't fuel new synthesis), plus glucocorticoids (and treat the trigger).

questions.stem: A patient with a known pituitary macroadenoma develops sudden 'worst headache of my life,' visual loss, an eye that won't move laterally, and hypotension. Besides hemodynamic support, what is the key medication?
questions.explain: Pituitary apoplexy (hemorrhage into the adenoma) can cause acute ACTH/cortisol deficiency and hypotension → give IV hydrocortisone with hemodynamic support; neurosurgery for decompression if vision is threatened.

questions.stem: A 3.5 cm adrenal mass with 8 HU unenhanced attenuation is found incidentally. The patient is asymptomatic. What is the appropriate next step?
options: Biochemical workup for hormone excess (and reassess malignancy risk)
questions.explain: Every adrenal incidentaloma needs biochemical screening for functionality (cortisol/Cushing's, metanephrines/pheo, aldosterone:renin if hypertensive). Low attenuation (<10 HU) and <4 cm are reassuring for a benign adenoma, but function must still be assessed.

questions.stem: A patient with suspected panhypopituitarism and central hypothyroidism is about to be started on hormone replacement. Which should be replaced first?
questions.explain: Replace glucocorticoids (hydrocortisone) before levothyroxine. Thyroid hormone speeds cortisol metabolism and raises demand; giving it first in a cortisol-deficient patient can precipitate adrenal crisis.

questions.stem: A patient with Addison's disease asks about managing a mild febrile respiratory infection at home. What is the correct sick-day rule?
options: Double or triple the hydrocortisone dose
questions.explain: Sick-day rule: double or triple the glucocorticoid for mild/moderate illness to match increased demand; if vomiting or unable to keep it down, go to the ER for IV steroids. Fludrocortisone provides mineralocorticoid replacement, not stress coverage.

cards.prompt: What visual field defect and pupillary sign suggest optic-chiasm compression by a pituitary mass?
cards.answer: Bitemporal hemianopsia plus a relative afferent pupillary defect (RAPD) on the swinging-flashlight test (both pupils dilate when light moves to the affected eye).

cards.prompt: Which secondary causes of hyperprolactinemia must be excluded before imaging?
cards.answer: Pregnancy (β-hCG), primary hypothyroidism (TRH stimulates prolactin), renal/liver dysfunction, and dopamine-antagonist drugs (metoclopramide, antipsychotics).

cards.prompt: What is first-line treatment for a symptomatic prolactinoma, even a macroadenoma?
cards.answer: A dopamine agonist (cabergoline, or bromocriptine if pregnancy desired) — it shrinks the tumour and restores fertility; surgery is reserved for failures/intolerance.

cards.prompt: How is acromegaly screened and confirmed?
cards.answer: Screen with IGF-1; confirm with failure of GH to suppress after an oral glucose load. MRI localizes after biochemical confirmation.

cards.prompt: What is the usual order of pituitary hormone loss?
cards.answer: GH → LH → FSH → TSH → ACTH → Prolactin ('Go Look For The Adenoma Please').

cards.prompt: How does radioiodine uptake separate causes of thyrotoxicosis?
cards.answer: High diffuse uptake = Graves'; focal hot = toxic adenoma/TMNG; low uptake = thyroiditis or exogenous hormone.

cards.prompt: Which findings are unique to Graves' disease?
cards.answer: Orbitopathy (proptosis), pretibial myxedema (dermopathy), and a thyroid bruit — from the stimulating TSH-receptor antibody.

cards.prompt: What thionamide side effect must patients be warned about, and what symptoms trigger action?
cards.answer: Agranulocytosis — fever or sore throat means stop the drug and get an urgent CBC. (PTU is also more hepatotoxic.)

cards.prompt: How should levothyroxine be taken?
cards.answer: Empty stomach in the morning, wait 30–60 min before food, and separate from iron/calcium by 3–4 hours; recheck TSH 6 weeks after a dose change.

cards.prompt: Why screen for adrenal insufficiency before starting levothyroxine?
cards.answer: Raising the metabolic rate increases cortisol demand; in unrecognized adrenal insufficiency this can precipitate a fatal adrenal crisis — replace cortisol first.

cards.prompt: How do you manage a hot vs a cold thyroid nodule?
cards.answer: A hot nodule is almost never cancer → no biopsy. A cold (photopenic) nodule carries malignancy risk → FNA if ultrasound features are suspicious.

cards.prompt: Why are patients with primary adrenal insufficiency hyperpigmented?
cards.answer: Low cortisol removes negative feedback → high ACTH; ACTH shares the POMC precursor with MSH and stimulates melanocortin receptors in skin.

cards.prompt: What confirms primary adrenal insufficiency?
cards.answer: An ACTH (Cortrosyn) stimulation test — a normal response is cortisol rising >500 nmol/L; failure to rise confirms the diagnosis. Baseline shows low cortisol with high ACTH/renin.

cards.prompt: What are the screening tests for Cushing syndrome (need 2 of 3)?
cards.answer: 24-h urine free cortisol, midnight salivary cortisol, and the 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression test (cortisol fails to suppress).

cards.prompt: How is pheochromocytoma diagnosed and prepared for surgery?
cards.answer: 24-h urinary metanephrines/catecholamines; pre-op alpha-blockade first (doxazosin/phenoxybenzamine), then beta-blockade, to prevent intraoperative crisis.

cards.prompt: Which endocrine emergencies include glucocorticoids in treatment?
cards.answer: Pituitary apoplexy, thyroid storm, myxedema coma, and adrenal crisis — when a stressed endocrine patient is unstable, steroids are rarely wrong.

cards.prompt: A prolactin level most strongly suggesting a true prolactinoma is:
Initial screening test for acromegaly:
Graves' disease shows what pattern on radioiodine uptake?
Preferred antithyroid drug in the first trimester of pregnancy:
After a high TSH, the confirmatory test for primary hypothyroidism is:
In confirmed Cushing's, a LOW ACTH indicates the source is:
Primary aldosteronism shows:
Correct pre-op blockade order for pheochromocytoma:
Which steroid stabilizes adrenal crisis without disturbing a pending cortisol assay?
The classic electrolyte pattern of Addison's disease is:
Select findings UNIQUE to Graves' disease:
Select accepted screening tests for Cushing syndrome:
Select skin/exam clues to a hereditary pheochromocytoma syndrome:
Select causes of thyrotoxicosis with LOW radioiodine uptake:
Select drugs that can cause hypothyroidism:

cards.explain: 'Salt, sugar, sex' from outer to inner; the medulla makes catecholamines.

cards.text: On the ACTH stimulation test, a normal response is cortisol rising above {{500}} nmol/L at 60 minutes.
cards.explain: Failure to rise confirms adrenal insufficiency.

cards.text: Acromegaly is confirmed when {{GH}} fails to suppress after an oral {{glucose}} load.
cards.explain: IGF-1 screens; the suppression test confirms.

cards.text: After a levothyroxine dose change, recheck {{TSH}} in {{6}} weeks; in pregnancy requirements rise ~{{30}}%.
cards.explain: Reflects the time to reach steady state.

cards.text: Pre-op pheochromocytoma blockade gives an {{alpha}}-blocker before a {{beta}}-blocker.
cards.explain: Beta-first risks unopposed alpha vasoconstriction → crisis.

cards.prompt: Order the workup of suspected thyrotoxicosis:
items: Confirm with free T4 / free T3

cards.prompt: Order thyroid-storm pharmacotherapy:
Order hormone replacement in central hypothyroidism + adrenal insufficiency:
Match the pituitary adenoma to its screening test/treatment:
Match the adrenal disorder to its hallmark lab:
pairs: Low cortisol, high ACTH; Na low/K high
High aldosterone, low renin; low K

cards.prompt: Match the emergency to its first move:
Match the drug to its action:
Distinguish the hook effect from the stalk effect.
cards.answer: Hook effect: a very high prolactin saturates the assay → falsely LOW report from a large prolactinoma. Stalk effect: a non-functioning mass compresses the stalk, blocking dopamine → mildly high prolactin (<200).

cards.prompt: How can hyperthyroidism present atypically in older adults?
cards.answer: As 'apathetic' hyperthyroidism — weight loss, atrial fibrillation, or depression rather than classic adrenergic features.

cards.prompt: What two questions must every adrenal incidentaloma answer?
cards.answer: Is it functional (screen for cortisol, catecholamine, and—if hypertensive—aldosterone excess)? And is it malignant (size >4 cm, attenuation >10 HU, heterogeneity raise concern)?

cards.prompt: What does a high TSH with a normal free T4 indicate?
cards.answer: Subclinical hypothyroidism. Treat if TSH ≥10, symptomatic, pregnant/planning pregnancy with positive anti-TPO, or a goiter; otherwise monitor.

cards.prompt: Subclinical hyperthyroidism most warrants treatment when there is:
options: A single low TSH during a viral illness

cards.prompt: Why avoid screening thyroid function in acutely ill inpatients?
cards.answer: Non-thyroidal illness lowers T3 (and later T4) with variable TSH, mimicking thyroid disease; you'll chase an artifact. Recheck after recovery if truly suspected.

cards.prompt: After water deprivation, giving desmopressin concentrates the urine. This indicates:
Name common causes of nephrogenic diabetes insipidus.
cards.answer: Lithium, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, and hereditary ADH-receptor/aquaporin defects — the kidney cannot respond to ADH.

cards.prompt: What defines SIADH biochemically, and how fast can you correct the sodium?
cards.answer: Euvolemic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine (urine osm > serum, urine Na >20–40), low serum osmolality, normal thyroid/adrenal/renal function. Correct ≤8–10 mmol/L per 24 h to avoid osmotic demyelination.

cards.text: Raise serum sodium by no more than ~{{8}}–{{10}} mmol/L per 24 h to avoid central pontine {{myelinolysis}}.
cards.explain: Overcorrection of chronic hyponatremia causes osmotic demyelination.

cards.prompt: MEN1 classically involves which trio?
What gene drives MEN2, and what must be excluded before any surgery?
cards.answer: The RET proto-oncogene. Always exclude (and treat first) pheochromocytoma before thyroid/parathyroid surgery — with alpha-then-beta blockade — to avoid intraoperative hypertensive crisis.

cards.prompt: Match the MEN syndrome to a defining feature:

guides.title: Week 26 · 1 The Pituitary Axis & Adenomas
guides.html:
Pituitary

The Pituitary Axis & Adenomas

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Mass effect and the cranial-nerve exam, the hyperfunctioning adenomas (prolactinoma, acromegaly), and how the gland fails.

Mass effect — what a sellar tumour compresses

StructureFinding
Optic chiasm (CN II)Bitemporal hemianopsia, ↓ acuity & red desaturation, a RAPD (swinging-flashlight: both pupils dilate when light moves to the affected eye), pale disc
Cavernous sinus (CN III, IV, VI)Diplopia, abnormal extra-ocular movements
CN V1/V2Forehead/cheek numbness
Stalk effectLoss of tonic dopamine inhibition → mildly ↑ prolactin + hypofunction of other axes

Prolactinoma / hyperprolactinemia

Prolactin suppresses GnRH pulsatility → central hypogonadism: women get oligo/amenorrhea, galactorrhea, infertility; men get low libido, ED.

Rule out secondary causes BEFORE imaging
Check β-hCG (pregnancy), TSH (hypothyroidism → high TRH stimulates prolactin), and renal/liver function. Stop dopamine antagonists (metoclopramide, antipsychotics). Med/hypothyroid causes are usually <100 µg/L; >250 µg/L suggests a true prolactinoma. MRI only after secondary causes excluded or with neuro symptoms.
  • Hook effect: a huge prolactin level overwhelms the assay → falsely low report from a large tumour.
  • Treatment: dopamine agonists (cabergoline, bromocriptine) shrink the tumour and restore fertility — first-line even for macroadenomas. Bromocriptine if pregnancy desired.

Acromegaly (GH excess)

Enlarging hands/feet/jaw (prognathism), carpal tunnel, OSA, arthritis, colonic polyps, diabetes/HTN. Screen with IGF-1; confirm with an oral glucose load that fails to suppress GH. Treat: transsphenoidal surgery; medical = somatostatin analogue (octreotide), GH-receptor antagonist (pegvisomant), or a dopamine agonist.

Hypopituitarism

Order of hormone loss: Go Look For The Adenoma Please (GH, LH, FSH, TSH, ACTH, Prolactin). Work up with 8 AM paired levels (TSH+fT4, ACTH+cortisol); stimulation tests if equivocal. Replace target hormones (levothyroxine, hydrocortisone, sex steroids, GH, DDAVP).

Bitemporal hemianopsia + a sellar mass = optic-chiasm compression. A mildly high prolactin with a big non-functioning mass = stalk effect; an unexpectedly low prolactin in a big tumour = hook effect.

guides.title: Week 26 · 2 Thyrotoxicosis & Hyperthyroidism
guides.html:
Overactive

Thyrotoxicosis & Hyperthyroidism

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

High vs low radioiodine uptake, Graves' unique signs, and the thionamides — including the pregnancy and agranulocytosis rules.

Thyrotoxicosis vs hyperthyroidism

Thyrotoxicosis = high circulating fT4/fT3 from any source. Hyperthyroidism = the gland over-producing. The radioiodine uptake (RAIU) scan splits them.

High uptake (gland making hormone)Low uptake (no new synthesis)
Graves' (diffuse) — TSH-receptor stimulating antibody; young womenSubacute thyroiditis — pre-formed hormone leak (painful post-viral, or painless post-partum)
Toxic adenoma / TMNG (focal "hot") — autonomous, older patientsExogenous thyroid hormone ingestion

Presentation

Weight loss with ↑ appetite, heat intolerance, tremor, palpitations, anxiety, hyperdefecation, proximal weakness. Graves'-specific: orbitopathy (proptosis), pretibial myxedema, thyroid bruit. Elderly may be "apathetic" or present only with atrial fibrillation.

Investigations

  • Screen: suppressed TSH. Confirm: high fT4 and fT3 (fT3 catches T3-toxicosis).
  • Etiology: RAIU scan — diffuse high = Graves'; focal hot = adenoma/TMNG; low = thyroiditis/exogenous.

Treatment

ToolRole
Beta-blocker (propranolol)Rapid symptom control (tremor, tachycardia) while definitive therapy works
Thionamides — methimazole (preferred), PTUInhibit thyroid peroxidase → block synthesis
Radioactive iodineAblates follicular cells; high rate of later hypothyroidism; contraindicated in pregnancy (need negative β-hCG)
Two thionamide rules that get tested
Agranulocytosis — rare but life-threatening: any fever/sore throat → stop drug, check CBC, urgent care. Pregnancy — methimazole is teratogenic in the first trimester, so use PTU early; PTU is more hepatotoxic.
Thyroiditis is self-limited: thionamides don't help (no synthesis happening) — treat with NSAIDs + a beta-blocker.

guides.title: Week 26 · 3 Hypothyroidism & Thyroid Nodules
guides.html:
Underactive & lumps

Hypothyroidism & Thyroid Nodules

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Hashimoto's and the levothyroxine rules (including the adrenal-crisis trap), plus the hot-vs-cold nodule workup.

Hypothyroidism

Causes: Hashimoto's (most common), post-RAI/surgery, iodine deficiency, drugs (lithium, amiodarone). Presentation: fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, dry skin, constipation, delayed reflex relaxation, bradycardia, diastolic HTN; elderly may present as confusion/failure-to-thrive.

  • Screen: elevated TSH. Confirm: low fT4. (Don't order fT3 — unreliable in hypothyroidism.)
  • Anti-TPO antibodies identify Hashimoto's etiology (not needed routinely if TSH clearly high).

Levothyroxine rules

RuleDetail
Dose~1.6 mcg/kg/day; recheck TSH in 6 weeks after changes
AdministrationEmpty stomach AM, wait 30–60 min before food; separate from iron/calcium by 3–4 h
Elderly / cardiacStart low (25–50 mcg), go slow
PregnancyRequirements rise ~30%
The adrenal-crisis trap
If adrenal insufficiency is suspected, screen/replace cortisol BEFORE starting levothyroxine — raising the metabolic rate increases cortisol demand and can precipitate a fatal adrenal crisis.

Thyroid nodules

  • Don't reflexively ultrasound abnormal labs unless there's a palpable abnormality.
  • If TSH is suppressed → RAIU scan. A "hot" nodule is almost never cancer — no biopsy.
  • A "cold" (photopenic) nodule carries malignancy risk → FNA if ultrasound features are suspicious.
Sequence for a thyrotoxic nodule: suppressed TSH → RAIU; a focal hot nodule explains the thyrotoxicosis and spares the patient a biopsy.

guides.title: Week 26 · 4 Adrenal Disorders
guides.html:
Adrenal

Adrenal Disorders

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Addison's vs Cushing's, the ACTH-splits-the-cause logic, Conn's and pheochromocytoma — including the alpha-before-beta rule.

Cortex anatomy (GFR → salt, sugar, sex)

Zona Glomerulosa → aldosterone; zona Fasciculata → cortisol; zona Reticularis → adrenal androgens. Medulla (neural crest) → catecholamines.

Primary adrenal insufficiency (Addison's)

  • Cause: usually autoimmune (often with Hashimoto's/vitiligo); also TB, HIV, hemorrhage.
  • Clues: fatigue, weight loss, hypotension; hyperpigmentation (high ACTH/MSH from POMC) in creases/scars/buccal mucosa. Labs: hyponatremia + hyperkalemia, hypoglycemia.
  • Confirm: low AM cortisol with high ACTH/renin; Cortrosyn (ACTH) stim — cortisol should rise >500 nmol/L.
Adrenal crisis
Don't wait for tests: IV saline + IV hydrocortisone. If the stim test isn't done yet, use dexamethasone (doesn't cross-react with the cortisol assay). Maintenance: hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone; sick-day rule = double/triple the steroid, ER if vomiting.

Cushing syndrome (hypercortisolism)

Most common cause = exogenous steroids. Endogenous: pituitary (Cushing's disease, high ACTH), adrenal tumour (low ACTH), or ectopic ACTH. Features: central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, purple striae, thin skin, proximal weakness, osteoporosis.

  • Confirm (need 2 of 3): 24-h urine free cortisol, midnight salivary cortisol, 1-mg overnight dexamethasone suppression (fails to suppress).
  • ACTH splits the cause: high/normal → pituitary or ectopic; low → adrenal.

Primary aldosteronism (Conn's)

Refractory HTN + hypokalemia; high aldosterone-to-renin ratio. Treat: adenoma → surgery; medical → aldosterone antagonist (spironolactone, eplerenone) or ENaC blocker (amiloride).

Pheochromocytoma

Triad: headache, palpitations, perspiration + paroxysmal HTN. Up to 50% germline (MEN2B — mucosal neuromas/marfanoid; NF1 — café-au-lait/neurofibromas). Diagnose with 24-h urinary metanephrines.

Alpha before beta
Pre-op: start an α-blocker (doxazosin / phenoxybenzamine) FIRST, then add a beta-blocker. Beta-blockade alone leaves unopposed α vasoconstriction → hypertensive crisis.

guides.title: Week 26 · 5 Endocrine Emergencies
guides.html:
Crises

Endocrine Emergencies

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The five endocrine emergencies of this week — recognition and the first moves for each.

The five to recognise instantly

EmergencyPictureFirst moves
Pituitary apoplexyHemorrhage into a macroadenoma: "worst headache," visual loss, CN palsies, hypotensionHemodynamic support, IV hydrocortisone, consider surgical decompression
Thyroid stormSevere thyrotoxicosis: fever, heart failure, vomiting, agitationBeta-blocker, PTU, iodine (after PTU), glucocorticoids
Myxedema comaSevere hypothyroidism: hypothermia, bradycardia, ↓ LOCIV thyroid hormone (T4±T3) + glucocorticoids
Adrenal (Addisonian) crisisShock precipitated by stress/illness in adrenal insufficiencyIV saline + IV hydrocortisone (dexamethasone if stim test pending)
Pheochromocytoma crisisHypertensive crisis on tumour manipulationPrevented by proper pre-op α-then-β blockade

Recurring rules

  • Glucocorticoids feature in three of these (apoplexy, storm, myxedema, crisis) — when in doubt about a stressed endocrine patient, cortisol is rarely wrong.
  • Order matters: in storm, give a beta-blocker + thionamide before iodine (iodine first could fuel synthesis); in pheo, alpha before beta.
  • Don't delay steroids for testing in adrenal crisis — use dexamethasone to preserve the assay.
A Hashimoto's patient started on levothyroxine who crashes into shock = unmasked Addisonian crisis: replace cortisol first.

guides.title: Week 26 · 6 Thyroid Function Tests & Subclinical Disease
guides.html:
TFT logic

Thyroid Function Tests & Subclinical Disease

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

Reading the TSH/fT4 grid, deciding when subclinical disease is worth treating, and not chasing the sick-euthyroid pattern.

The TSH / free-T4 grid

TSHfT4Interpretation
HighLowOvert primary hypothyroidism
HighNormalSubclinical hypothyroidism
LowHighOvert hyperthyroidism
LowNormalSubclinical hyperthyroidism
LowLowCentral hypothyroidism or sick-euthyroid
HighHighTSH-secreting adenoma or thyroid hormone resistance (rare)

TSH is the most sensitive screen because of the log-linear feedback: a small fT4 change moves TSH a lot.

Subclinical hypothyroidism — treat when?

Treat subclinical hypothyroidism if
TSH ≥ 10 mIU/L, OR symptomatic, OR positive anti-TPO with intent to conceive / pregnant, OR a goiter. Otherwise (TSH 4–10, asymptomatic) usually monitor, since many normalize and over-treatment causes harm.

Subclinical hyperthyroidism

Suppressed TSH with normal fT4/fT3. Treat when TSH is persistently <0.1, especially with atrial fibrillation, osteoporosis, or age >65 (cardiac and bone risk). Confirm it is persistent and not thyroiditis first.

Sick-euthyroid (non-thyroidal illness)

  • In acute illness: low T3 first (impaired peripheral conversion), then low T4 in severe illness; TSH often low-normal and rebounds on recovery.
  • Don't screen thyroid function in acutely ill inpatients unless thyroid disease is genuinely suspected — you'll chase an artifact. Recheck after recovery.
TSH first; add fT4 to place the patient on the grid. Subclinical hypothyroidism: treat at TSH ≥10 or if symptomatic/pregnant. A low T3 in a sick inpatient is usually sick-euthyroid, not thyroid disease.

guides.title: Week 26 · 7 Posterior Pituitary: Diabetes Insipidus & SIADH
guides.html:
Water balance

Posterior Pituitary: Diabetes Insipidus & SIADH

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The two ADH disorders — too little (DI) and too much (SIADH) — how to localize them, and how to correct sodium safely.

ADH in one line

ADH (vasopressin) makes the collecting duct reabsorb water. Too little → dilute urine and rising sodium (DI); too much → water retention and falling sodium (SIADH).

Diabetes insipidus

Central DINephrogenic DI
DefectNo ADH (pituitary/hypothalamic)Kidney unresponsive to ADH
CausesTumour, surgery, trauma, apoplexyLithium, hypercalcemia, hypokalemia, hereditary
PicturePolyuria + polydipsia, dilute urine, hypernatremia if water not accessible
Water deprivation test then desmopressin
Deprive water: in DI the urine stays dilute. Then give desmopressin (DDAVP): central DI concentrates the urine (responds); nephrogenic DI does not (kidney can't respond). Central DI → treat with desmopressin; nephrogenic → remove the cause (stop lithium), thiazide, low-salt diet.

SIADH

  • Euvolemic hyponatremia with concentrated urine (urine osm > serum osm, urine Na >20–40), low serum osmolality, normal thyroid/adrenal/renal function.
  • Causes: CNS disease, pulmonary disease, malignancy (small-cell lung), drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine).
  • Treat: fluid restriction first; severe/symptomatic → hypertonic saline; persistent → vaptan (tolvaptan) or demeclocycline.
Correct sodium slowly
Raise serum sodium by no more than ~8–10 mmol/L in 24 h. Overcorrection of chronic hyponatremia risks osmotic demyelination (central pontine myelinolysis).
DDAVP separates the DIs: central responds, nephrogenic doesn't. SIADH is euvolemic hyponatremia with inappropriately concentrated urine; fix it slowly.

guides.title: Week 26 · 8 Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN)
guides.html:
Inherited tumours

Multiple Endocrine Neoplasia (MEN)

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite

The three MEN patterns, the genes behind them, and the screening that prevents lethal complications.

Why MEN matters

MEN syndromes cluster endocrine tumours in families. Recognizing one tumour should prompt screening for the others — and for at-risk relatives — because some components (medullary thyroid cancer, pheochromocytoma) are lethal if missed.

SyndromeGeneComponents (mnemonic)
MEN 1MEN1 (menin)"3 Ps": Parathyroid hyperplasia, Pancreatic islet tumours (gastrinoma, insulinoma), Pituitary adenoma
MEN 2ARETMedullary thyroid cancer, Pheochromocytoma, Parathyroid hyperplasia
MEN 2BRETMedullary thyroid cancer, Pheochromocytoma, mucosal neuromas / marfanoid habitus (no parathyroid)

Key clinical points

  • Medullary thyroid cancer arises from parafollicular C cells → secretes calcitonin (tumour marker). RET-positive carriers are offered prophylactic thyroidectomy.
  • Always exclude pheochromocytoma BEFORE any surgery in MEN2 (operate on the pheo first, with alpha-then-beta blockade) — unrecognized pheo causes intraoperative hypertensive crisis.
  • Primary hyperparathyroidism is the most common and earliest manifestation of MEN1.
Order of operations in MEN2
Pheochromocytoma is resected (or at least medically blocked) before thyroid or parathyroid surgery. The same alpha-before-beta rule from the adrenal guide applies.
MEN1 = 3 Ps (parathyroid, pancreas, pituitary; MEN1 gene). MEN2 = medullary thyroid cancer + pheo (RET gene); 2B adds mucosal neuromas/marfanoid and drops the parathyroid. Find a pheo before you operate.

guides.title: Week 26 · 9 Cram Sheet
guides.html:
Night-Before High-Yield

Endocrine II

Week 26 · Endocrine II — Pituitary, Thyroid & Adrenal
CPC 2 · Year 1
Study Suite
★ Do-not-miss · first-step reflexes
Adrenal (Addisonian) crisis → IV saline + IV hydrocortisone NOW (dexamethasone if a stim test is pending); don't wait for results
Pituitary apoplexy (worst headache + visual loss + CN palsy + hypotension) → IV hydrocortisone + decompression
Thyroid storm → beta-blocker + PTU, then iodine, + glucocorticoids
Myxedema coma (hypothermia, bradycardia, ↓LOC) → IV thyroid hormone + glucocorticoid
Pheochromocytoma → ALPHA-blocker before beta-blocker (beta-first = unopposed α crisis)
Levothyroxine in unrecognized adrenal insufficiency → can precipitate adrenal crisis — replace cortisol FIRST

Pituitary Pituitary

Chiasm compression bitemporal hemianopsia + RAPD
Prolactinoma Rx dopamine agonist (cabergoline); >250 µg/L = true tumour
Acromegaly screen IGF-1; confirm GH non-suppression on OGTT
Hook effect huge prolactin → falsely LOW assay
Stalk effect non-functioning mass, mild prolactin <200
Hormone-loss order GH, LH, FSH, TSH, ACTH, PRL

Hyperthyroid Thyroid+

Screen / confirm suppressed TSH; high fT4/fT3
RAIU high Graves (diffuse) / toxic nodule (focal)
RAIU low thyroiditis / exogenous → supportive only
Graves-only signs orbitopathy, pretibial myxedema, bruit
Thionamides methimazole preferred; PTU in 1st trimester
Agranulocytosis fever/sore throat → stop + CBC

Hypothyroid & nodules Thyroid-

Screen / confirm high TSH; low fT4 (don't order fT3)
Levothyroxine AM empty stomach; recheck TSH 6 wk; pregnancy +30%
Elderly/cardiac start low, go slow
Adrenal trap replace cortisol before levothyroxine
Hot nodule almost never cancer → no FNA
Cold nodule FNA if suspicious

Adrenal Adrenal

Addison's labs Na low, K high; hyperpigmentation (ACTH/MSH)
Addison's confirm ACTH stim — cortisol rise >500
Cushing's screen 2 of 3: UFC, midnight saliva, 1-mg DST
Cushing's ACTH low = adrenal; high/normal = pituitary/ectopic
Conn's refractory HTN + low K; high aldo:renin
Pheo 24-h metanephrines; alpha-then-beta
Sick-day rule double/triple hydrocortisone; ER if vomiting

TFTs & subclinical TFTs

Screen TSH first; add fT4 to localize
Subclinical hypo high TSH, normal fT4
Treat subclinical hypo TSH ≥10, symptomatic, pregnant/anti-TPO
Subclinical hyper low TSH, normal fT4/fT3
Treat subclinical hyper TSH <0.1 + AF/osteoporosis/age>65
Sick-euthyroid low T3 in illness — don't screen inpatients

Posterior pituitary ADH

Central DI no ADH → responds to DDAVP
Nephrogenic DI kidney resistant (lithium, ↑Ca) → no DDAVP response
DI picture polyuria, dilute urine, hypernatremia
SIADH euvolemic hyponatremia, concentrated urine
SIADH treat fluid restriction; hypertonic saline if severe; vaptan
Correction limit ≤8–10 mmol/L/24 h (avoid osmotic demyelination)

MEN syndromes MEN

MEN1 (MEN1 gene) 3 Ps: parathyroid, pancreas, pituitary
MEN2A (RET) medullary thyroid Ca, pheo, parathyroid
MEN2B (RET) MTC, pheo, mucosal neuromas/marfanoid
MTC marker calcitonin; prophylactic thyroidectomy in carriers
Before surgery exclude/treat pheo FIRST (alpha-then-beta)
